title: How to Choose Between Long-form and Short-form Scripts
metaTitle: How to Choose Between Long-form and Short-form Scripts | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to choosing long-form or short-form Creator Library materials without treating one format as a shortened version of the other.
excerpt: A practical guide to deciding whether a Creator Library topic should become a long-form video, a short-form video, or both as separate productions.
deck: Long-form and short-form scripts serve different jobs inside the Creator Library. This guide explains how to choose the right production path before opening the Scene workspace.

sections.title: Start with the viewing context
paragraphs: Long-form is built for viewers who want the full shape of a mystery. It gives room for background, uncertainty, atmosphere, and gradual escalation.
Short-form is built for viewers who need one clear idea quickly. It should feel complete on its own, not like a trimmed preview of a longer video.

sections.title: Choose long-form when the story needs room
paragraphs: Use the long-form version when the subject depends on context. Legends with disputed origins, layered motifs, multiple versions, or source boundaries usually need more space.
A strong long-form script does not simply add more explanation. It lets the viewer move through the mystery one Scene at a time.

sections.title: Choose short-form when the core image is clear
paragraphs: Use the short-form version when one image, object, location, or question can carry the whole piece. The viewer should understand the premise almost immediately.
A good short-form script keeps only the pressure point of the story. It moves quickly, but it still has a beginning, a turn, and a final beat.

sections.title: Use both with different intentions
paragraphs: The same archive record can support both formats. The important thing is to treat each version as its own production.
Long-form should guide the viewer through the record. Short-form should capture the strongest single moment without flattening the story.

title: How to Use Scene Workspaces Without Losing the Story
metaTitle: How to Use Scene Workspaces Without Losing the Story | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to using Scene workspaces as production units while keeping the original mystery clear and coherent.
excerpt: A guide to reading each Scene as a complete production unit without losing the larger narrative thread.
deck: Scene workspaces are built for making video, but each Scene still belongs to a larger story. This guide explains how to follow the workflow without breaking the narrative.

sections.title: Treat each Scene as a work unit
paragraphs: A Scene is the smallest production unit in the Creator Library. It gathers narration, visual focus, prompts, music direction, and editing guidance in one place.
This prevents the creator from jumping between unrelated sections while building a single moment of the video.

sections.title: Keep the Scene Role visible
paragraphs: Scene Role explains what the moment does inside the video. It may hook the viewer, introduce a legend, build doubt, present evidence, or leave a final question.
Before producing the assets, check the role. It keeps the Scene from becoming a pretty image with no narrative job.

sections.title: Move through the fields in order
paragraphs: Start with the narration. Then read the voice direction and Scene Focus before copying the image prompt.
That order matters because the image should support the spoken story, not pull the video into a different mood.

sections.title: Check the transition before moving on
paragraphs: A Scene is not finished when the image is created. It is finished when the viewer can move naturally from this moment to the next one.
Use the Editing Guide and optional advanced notes to make the end of the Scene feel connected to the next beat.

title: How Creator Toolkit Fits Into the Production Flow
metaTitle: How Creator Toolkit Fits Into the Production Flow | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to using Creator Toolkit as preparation before working through Long-form or Short-form Scenes.
excerpt: A guide to understanding Creator Toolkit as the preparation area before building video Scenes.
deck: Creator Toolkit is not a tutorial hub or a service recommendation list. It is the preparation shelf that tells creators what they need before building the video.

sections.title: Prepare before you create
paragraphs: Creator Toolkit appears before the Scene work begins because it answers a simple question: what should be ready before production starts?
It covers images, narration, background music, editing, and optional motion at a glance.

sections.title: Examples are not requirements
paragraphs: The Toolkit may name common tools or search directions, but those names are examples. The Creator Library does not require one specific service or workflow.
The goal is to help the creator understand the type of asset needed, then use whatever tool fits their process.

sections.title: The Scenes provide the real material
paragraphs: Toolkit stays short because the actual production material lives inside each Scene. The Scene contains the narration, visual focus, prompt, music keywords, and editing flow.
That separation keeps preparation simple and production focused.

sections.title: Use Toolkit as a final readiness check
paragraphs: Before starting a video, scan the five Toolkit areas once. If each area makes sense, the creator can move into Long-form or Short-form production without searching for another guide.
The Toolkit is deliberately brief. It should make the next action clearer, not slow the work down.

title: Why Story Summary Comes Before Production Assets
metaTitle: Why Story Summary Comes Before Production Assets | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide explaining why every Creator Library page begins with the story before moving into production materials.
excerpt: A guide to the Story area and why production should begin with understanding the record.
deck: The Creator Library is a production space, but it still begins with the story. This order keeps the video grounded before prompts and editing choices begin.

sections.title: Production starts with understanding
paragraphs: A video can only stay coherent if the creator understands the story first. Story Summary gives the subject, mood, conflict, and source boundary before any assets are created.
This prevents the production from chasing visuals that feel dramatic but do not match the record.

sections.title: Story Information sets the production frame
paragraphs: Story Information works like a quick production brief. It identifies genre, motif, setting, mood, difficulty, and recommended length.
Those details guide the creator's pacing and tone before the first Scene begins.

sections.title: Shared context prevents repetition
paragraphs: Long-form and Short-form both use the same Story area. That keeps common information from being repeated in two different places.
The production sections can then focus on what each format needs to make the video work.

sections.title: Assets come after the record is clear
paragraphs: Image prompts, music keywords, and editing notes make better sense after the creator knows what the story is doing.
The Story area is not a delay. It is the anchor that keeps the production from drifting away from the mystery.

title: How to Read Scene Focus Before Writing Visual Prompts
metaTitle: How to Read Scene Focus Before Writing Visual Prompts | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to using Scene Focus as the director's intent before copying or adapting an Image Prompt.
excerpt: A guide to understanding Scene Focus as the production intention behind the Image Prompt.
deck: Scene Focus tells the creator what the viewer should notice or feel before the Image Prompt supplies the full visual instruction.

sections.title: Scene Focus is the intention
paragraphs: Scene Focus is not a short version of the prompt. It explains the role of the image in the viewer's experience.
It may point to doubt, isolation, discovery, danger, or a small detail that changes the meaning of the Scene.

sections.title: Image Prompts translate that intention
paragraphs: The Image Prompt turns the focus into a concrete scene. It describes space, subject, light, mood, color, camera feeling, and realism.
Reading Scene Focus first helps the creator judge whether the generated image supports the story instead of only looking attractive.

sections.title: Use Focus to reject the wrong image
paragraphs: A generated image can be technically good and still wrong for the Scene. If it misses the Scene Focus, it should be revised.
The question is not only whether the image looks mysterious. The question is whether it makes the viewer understand the right moment.

sections.title: Keep the visual choice specific
paragraphs: Strong mystery visuals usually depend on one clear subject. A crowded image can weaken the story because the viewer does not know where to look.
Scene Focus helps narrow the image before the prompt expands it.

title: How Background Music Keywords Shape a Mystery Video
metaTitle: How Background Music Keywords Shape a Mystery Video | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to reading background music keywords as mood direction rather than track recommendations.
excerpt: A guide to using background music keywords to support narration without overwhelming the story.
deck: Creator Library music guidance is written as search language, not as a required track list. The keywords help creators find a mood that fits the Scene.

sections.title: Music should support the voice
paragraphs: In a mystery video, background music should leave space for narration. If the music competes with the voice, the story becomes harder to follow.
The best track is usually the one the viewer feels before they notice it.

sections.title: Keywords describe the mood
paragraphs: Terms like Dark Ambient, Low Drone, Suspense Piano, and Mystery Atmosphere are search directions. They describe emotional texture, not one specific source.
The creator can use these terms in any music library, editor, or generation tool that fits their workflow.

sections.title: Match the Scene, not the whole video
paragraphs: A video may need more than one mood. The opening might need quiet curiosity, while the middle might need tension and the ending might need restraint.
Scene-level music keywords help each moment carry its own weight.

sections.title: Avoid overstating the mystery
paragraphs: Music should not force fear onto a story. Kyunolab works best when tension comes from the record, the image, and the unanswered question.
A restrained track often keeps the mystery more believable.

title: How Advanced Production Notes Should Stay Optional
metaTitle: How Advanced Production Notes Should Stay Optional | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to using Advanced Production notes without letting them interrupt the basic Scene workflow.
excerpt: A guide to treating advanced production information as optional support after the basic Scene is complete.
deck: Advanced Production exists to improve a Scene, not to make the basic workflow harder. This guide explains why it stays folded until the creator wants more detail.

sections.title: Basic production must work first
paragraphs: Every Scene should be usable with the visible fields alone. Narration, Scene Focus, Image Prompt, music keywords, and Editing Guide must be enough to produce the moment.
Advanced Production is for creators who want extra refinement after that basic structure is clear.

sections.title: Folded notes protect the workflow
paragraphs: Keeping advanced notes collapsed prevents the page from overwhelming beginners. It also helps experienced creators move quickly through the core Scene.
The button makes extra detail available without pushing it into the main production path.

sections.title: Motion and sound are not always needed
paragraphs: Some Scenes benefit from motion prompts, sound effects, or transition notes. Others are stronger when they stay quiet and still.
Optional information should match the Scene instead of repeating the same suggestion everywhere.

sections.title: Advanced does not mean complicated
paragraphs: Advanced Production should remain practical. It may describe a slow push-in, a faint room tone, or a simple color direction.
The goal is better control, not professional complexity.

title: How Creator Library Categories Organize Script Packages
metaTitle: How Creator Library Categories Organize Script Packages | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to understanding Creator Library categories as production shelves rather than archive categories.
excerpt: A guide to reading Creator Library categories as script-package shelves for video production.
deck: Creator Library categories help creators find production-ready script packages. They are connected to the archive, but they are not the same as archive reading categories.

sections.title: Categories act like production shelves
paragraphs: In the Creator Library, a category is a shelf of script packages. It helps the creator find topics by the kind of video they want to make.
Urban Legends, Internet Folklore, Myths, and Mythic Objects may all need different pacing, visuals, and tone.

sections.title: Category does not change the template
paragraphs: Every category still uses the same production structure. Story comes first, then Toolkit, Workflow, Long-form Creator, Short-form Creator, and reference material.
That consistency lets creators move between topics without learning a new page format each time.

sections.title: The category sets expectations
paragraphs: A category helps the creator anticipate the kind of assets they may need. A haunted place may lean on atmosphere, while a mythic object may need a clear central prop.
The Scene details still make the final decisions, but the category gives the first production direction.

sections.title: Archive and Library stay separate
paragraphs: Archive categories organize reading records. Library categories organize creator materials.
The two systems can point to each other, but they serve different jobs.

title: How Original Archive Records and Creator Scripts Stay Separate
metaTitle: How Original Archive Records and Creator Scripts Stay Separate | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide explaining the boundary between Kyunolab Archive records and Creator Library production pages.
excerpt: A guide to the boundary between reading records and creator-facing production materials.
deck: Kyunolab keeps Archive records and Creator Library scripts connected but separate. This guide explains why that boundary matters for readers and creators.

sections.title: Archive is for reading
paragraphs: The Mystery Archive presents the record as a reading experience. It focuses on story, source status, meaning, atmosphere, and related archive paths.
It does not need to behave like a production checklist.

sections.title: Creator Library is for making
paragraphs: The Creator Library turns selected archive material into production-ready assets. It gives narration, prompts, music direction, editing guidance, and optional advanced notes.
Its purpose is not just to explain the story. Its purpose is to help someone make a finished video.

sections.title: Connections should be clear, not blended
paragraphs: A script page may link back to the original record, but it should not replace that record. The reading page and production page should each keep their own role.
That separation helps the site stay understandable as both the Archive and the Creator Library grow.

sections.title: The boundary protects trust
paragraphs: Legends, folklore, mysteries, and invented narrative treatments can easily blur together. Clear page roles help prevent that confusion.
The Archive preserves the record. The Library adapts the record for production.

title: How to Build a Complete Video From One Script Page
metaTitle: How to Build a Complete Video From One Script Page | Library Board
metaDescription: A Library Board guide to following one Creator Library page from Story through Finish to complete a mystery video.
excerpt: A guide to using one Creator Library script page as a complete path from story understanding to finished video.
deck: A Creator Library page is designed to carry the creator from understanding the story to assembling the final video. This guide explains the intended flow.

sections.title: Begin with Story
paragraphs: Start by reading Story Summary and Story Information. This gives the topic, mood, core motif, setting, and production difficulty before any assets are made.
The first step is not copying a prompt. The first step is knowing what kind of video the story wants to become.

sections.title: Prepare the basic assets
paragraphs: Creator Toolkit shows the asset types to prepare: images, narration, background music, editing, and optional motion.
Production Workflow then turns that preparation into a simple order of work.

sections.title: Create Scene by Scene
paragraphs: Choose Long-form Creator or Short-form Creator. Then work through the Scenes in order.
Each Scene gives the narration first, then the visual and editing material needed to produce that moment.

sections.title: Finish with the reference area
paragraphs: After production, use the Finish area for the original story, usage note, and related script paths.
The page is complete when the creator can understand the source, make the video, and know where the material came from.
